data.title: CLOB: Not A Good Time To Own CLO Tranches (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We first started covering the VanEck AA-BB CLO ETF (CLOB) a year ago, when we praised the fund for bringing the structure and risk profile of a vertical slice of a CLO to market. However, we did not feel bullish in terms of forward price appreciation for the ETF, so we assigned it a 'Hold' rating. The fund is fairly flat from a price perspective, with a modest total return.

In today's article we are going to revisit CLOB, especially in light of the current macro developments that favor fixed rate assets over floating rate ones. We will show readers why we are a 'Sell' for this ETF into 2026.

The structure is good

What we still like about CLOB is its structure, which is a vertical slice of a CLO. This purely means that the name blends a number of CLO tranches in one fund:

  • AA: 42%
  • A: 23%
  • BBB: 21%
  • BB: 14%

The market already has specific tranche ETFs, such as the Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF (JBBB) geared towards BBB CLOs, and the Eldridge BBB-B CLO ETF (CLOZ) targeting BB names. We have articles covering both those ETFs, and we have 'Sell' ratings associated with each one.

As a reminder, a CLO tranche looks as follows:

By virtue of building an ETF with various tranches, CLOB is like a vertical slice out of a CLO. Think about it as a cake, where a user slices a piece for consumption. CLOB is therefore more of a take on the entire asset class rather than a specific tranche. If we get a recession, BB names will underperform and possibly default, depending on the severity of such a recession. This means CLOB will suffer from a price perspective.

Similarly, in such a scenario, BBB tranches will also experience downward price pressure, but will not default given their subordination. The point is that an investor has no buffer here via CLOB, since they own pieces of each risk tranche as per the graph above.

Floating rate assets are coming under pressure

We are in a macro environment where rates are forecasted to come down significantly. The Fed is set to cut aggressively in 2026, and this will result in lower yields for CLOB. Currently the 30-day SEC yield is 6.2%, but it is set to move lower with Fed Funds and SOFR.

Remember that leveraged loans are set on SOFR, which in turn gets its levels from Fed Funds. Once Fed Funds move lower, SOFR follows, but with a lag (depending on tenor). Ultimately, SOFR correlates one-to-one with the Fed cuts.

The futures market currently gives us the following picture in terms of SOFR outlook:

The market is currently expecting a neutral rate close to 3%, which means another 70 bps lower in Fed Funds. One can extrapolate to a forward yield for the ETF of 5.5%, with an implied lag to Fed Funds.

In this macro environment, investors should not want to be in floating rate assets but fixed ones, especially for longer weighted average life holdings.

The fund is sensitive to credit spreads

Despite lacking a duration profile due to the floating-rate nature of the holdings, the fund does have credit sensitivity when it comes to spreads:

During the April 2025 market sell-off, we saw a -5% drawdown in the ETF, driven by spreads. In an actual recession this could go much lower, closer to -7% to -9%, depending on the severity of such a theoretical recession.

The issue currently facing the CLO asset class is two fold:

  1. SOFR is moving lower, thus lowering all-in yields
  2. Credit spreads are very tight historically

While we already outlined the forward for the rates market, let us take a look at some CLO spreads in historic context:

You can notice from the chart above that spreads (called discount margin in the chart above) are close to historic lows. This simply means that prices in turn are close to historic highs, and they are prone to downdrafts during sell-off events. You want to buy CLOs when spreads are wide and market participants shun the asset class, not when they are the most popular girl at the ball.

Some fund analytics

Let us also look at some of the fund's analytics here:

  • AUM: $0.15 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.45%
  • 30-day SEC Yield: 6.2%
  • Portfolio WAL: 1.8 years
  • Portfolio WA spread: 250 bps

The fund is indeed constructed fairly conservatively, with a low weighted average life of 1.8 years, and an acceptable expense ratio for the underlying asset class. Please remember the ETF is an active one; thus, risk metrics can change as the managers see fit for the underlying market conditions.

Conclusion

CLOB is an active ETF from VanEck, focused on a quasi-vertical slice of a CLO. The name is overweight AA and BBB tranches, and comes with an expense ratio of 0.45%. The fund will suffer as the Fed cuts, with the underlying asset class being floating rate. Furthermore, we find CLO spreads to be very tight, which means the next move during a risk-off event will be lower. We still like the fund structuring and what it does, but the macro environment is not attractive for this type of risk. We are a 'Sell' for CLOB in today's macro context.

data.title: Groupon: Focus On Billings Growth Recovery (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The S&P 500 has managed to hold near to record highs so far in 2026, but the mood in the markets appears to be anything but calm. With a vast array of potential downside macro catalysts against us (a shaky consumer economy, rising geopolitical tensions, a continued assault against Fed Chair Powell), investors are bracing for the next major leg downward.

My strategy to protect myself from downside remains the same: bias my portfolio exposure to more value-oriented stocks, particularly in the small and mid-cap space. Groupon (GRPN), in my view, is finally looking attractive after the stock took a recent dive despite strong results. The stock is still up ~40% over the past year, but down by more than half relative to mid-summer highs above $35. In my view, it's a great time for investors to reassess the bull case in this name again.

I last wrote a neutral article on Groupon in September, when the stock was trading at $23 per share. Since then, a combination of a ~30% share price decline plus continued strong billings performance have driven me to rethink my outlook on this stock. Given cheap valuation multiples and the opportunity for a stock market rotation out of large caps and into small, I'm upgrading Groupon to a buy.

Since an incredibly attractive valuation is the core reason to buy this stock, let's first start with a discussion of Groupon's latest valuation multiples. At current share prices near $16, Groupon trades at a market cap of $657.8 million. After we net off the $313.3 million of cash and investments on Groupon's latest balance sheet against $310.1 million of debt, the company's resulting enterprise value is $654.6 million.

For the remainder of FY25, Groupon has maintained its full-year billings guidance at 7-9% y/y growth, while raising full-year revenue expectations to $500-$505 million, or 2-3% y/y growth versus a wider $497-$505 million (1-3% range) previously. And while the company has moderated down its adjusted EBITDA expectations slightly (to $70-$72 million, from $70-$75 million previously), it is raising its FCF expectation to $60 million or a 12% midpoint margin, versus "at least $50 million" expected previously

For next year FY26, Wall Street is expecting revenue growth to more closely line up to billings growth, with consensus pointing to $556.5 million in revenue next year (+11% y/y growth). If we conservatively assume current-year adjusted EBITDA and FCF margins of 14.1% and 11.9% hold, respectively, adjusted EBITDA and FCF next year would be $78.5 million and $66.2 million.

This positions Groupon's valuation multiples at:

  • 9.2x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA and 8.3x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA
  • 10.9x EV/FY25 FCF and 9.9x EV/FY26 FCF

For a company that is expecting to return to ~10% revenue and billings growth with the additional cherry on top of consistent margin expansion, these are quite modest valuation multiples to pay. We add on top of this that Groupon is taking heavy advantage of its current share price slide by dedicating its ample cash resources toward buybacks. The company has waited to use up its years-old share repurchase authorization; it has $245 million left on this program, worth a whopping ~third of the company's current market cap. This buyback is also fully fundable from the company's existing balance sheet cash, not to mention ongoing growth in free cash flow.

Stay long here: Groupon is priced at a low enough level to enable a near-term rebound, especially with solid top-line performance underfoot.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Groupon's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Groupon's revenue grew 7% y/y to $122.8 million, slightly ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $122.0 million (+6% y/y).

The chart below, meanwhile, breaks out performance in the company's largest geographic segment of North America. As a reminder, billings represents actual customer purchases of local deals, while Groupon's revenue reflects when deals and offers are actually consumed. As such, billings is the most important forward-looking indictor of Groupon's growth trajectory.

North American billings grew 18% y/y to $294 million, continuing a three-quarter trend of re-acceleration to double-digit growth. Now, there were puts and takes in the quarter. Groupon notes that SMB (small and medium-sized businesses) continue to be the heart of the company's revenue base, and that revenue from SMB offers accelerated in Q3. That said, larger enterprise brands saw a slowdown in growth, which the company attributed to weakness in a number of enterprise campaigns.

That said, this isn't a cause yet for concern. Groupon continues to execute its hyper-local sales strategy, chasing supply in select attractive markets. For example, the company recently reallocated a lot of its sales headcount to Chicago, and in turn saw billings in the Chicago metro area grow at double the rate of the North America business (implying mid-30s growth). The company intends to keep driving a local-first sales approach going forward.

On the demand side of the house, the company notes that discovery stemming from AI search engines is growing. Per the company's commentary on the Q3 shareholder letter:

In Q2, we highlighted the early momentum we were seeing in AI-powered search traffic. This is a dynamic environment with frequent algorithm changes, but our platform is navigating these changes well, and we saw AI search traffic tracking toward all time highs in September. While this remains a small portion of our overall traffic today and did not have a significant impact on our Q3 financial results, the trajectory is encouraging. In recent months, we've increased our visibility to AI crawlers and large language model indexing through structured data optimization. We're building the technical infrastructure required for AI native commerce, including Modern API architecture, AI-ready search & relevance and AI-ready checkout."

Meanwhile, international also showed strong performance. Excluding the company's exit from Italy, the company's billings grew 15% y/y. Similar to the U.S., revenue growth also accelerated here to 8% y/y growth on a normalized basis.

Groupon also delivered $17.5 million in adjusted EBITDA, rising 18% y/y and hitting a 14.3% margin that improved 140bps versus 12.9% in the year-ago quarter.

Key takeaways

It's quite evident from recent performance that Groupon's strategy of selectively chasing supply in high-growth markets while emphasizing a localized sales force is working. Billings and revenue are all on an uptrend, providing tailwinds for adjusted EBITDA. We're more than happy to take a position in this stock at ~10x FCF.

data.title: Delek: Sum Of The Parts Points To Substantial Upside
data.description:
Shares of Delek US Holdings (DK) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining 52%, as regulatory activities have greatly improved the company’s financial position. That said, shares have dropped about 25% from their recent highs, as focus has shifted from regulatory benefits to a weakening of the macro refining environment. Recent geopolitical actions from Venezuela to Iran have further increased intrigue around the sector. I last covered shares in November when I downgraded them from a “strong buy” to a “buy.” With the stock down 20% since then, I should have downgraded them even more significantly. Given recent developments and this underperformance, now is a good time to revisit Delek.

Refining spreads have narrowed

Looking first at the macro environment, the primary driver of the decline in Delek (and many of the refining stocks) is quite clear. As you can see below, crack spreads (which measure how much refiners make turning crude oil into products like gasoline and diesel) have plunged in recent weeks and are now in the low $20s, after having passed $30. While I was a bit surprised how wide spreads become given it was a quiet hurricane season, the magnitude of the retrenchment has been equally surprising. I view ~$25 as the long-term norm for refining margins, so we have swung from an environment that is clearly favorable to refiners to one that is currently unfavorable.

Crack spreads are driven by the relative availability of crude vs refined products, and we have seen a dramatic increase in gasoline inventory. As you can see below, a few months ago, gasoline inventories were at 5-year lows. While they typically rise during the winter as demand slows, the magnitude of this rise has been jarring, so much so that inventories are now at 5-year highs. This points to there being a fairly significant slowdown in demand, which has raised concerns about the product market being oversupplied and keeping spreads narrow.

Now, inventories and demand can be particularly volatile around the holiday season, and my view is that the US economy is likely to grow modestly (~2%) in 2026, which should support stable demand over the course of the year and keep product inventories from rising too much. Beyond this, we should see some support on the crude supply side, given the government change in Venezuela.

Venezuelan production has plunged over the past decade as underinvestment and sanctions have crippled its productive capacity. I do not expect production to return back to mid-2010 levels anytime soon, as that will likely require years of investment. That said, efforts to re-route previously sanctioned oil into the US to be refined (given our expertise at refining heavy oils) should increase the domestic supply of crude modestly, helping to put a floor under crack spreads. Over the medium term, more Venezuelan production adds to my view that $25 is a normalized level for crack spreads.

EPA action provides material benefit to Delek

Apart from this significant shift in the macro environment, Delek is a significant winner of regulatory actions. In the company’s third quarter reported back on November 7th, Delek reported an outsized $7.13, well ahead of estimates. However, this included a one-time gain from the favorable resolution of its petitions to the EPA for relief from renewable fuel standards under the small refinery exception (“SRE”). The SRE allows companies like Delek to be relieved of their RFS burden.

By mid-2026, Delek expects to receive $400 million in cash from this decision. As a reminder, this windfall represents payments from prior years 2019-2024. Delek received full approval of its 2023-2024 petitions, but only a partial approval for 2019-2022. The company plans to pursue additional compensation for those years. Beyond this, Delek expects 100% of its refining output with qualify from the SRE going forward, significantly improving ongoing cash flow. This will be a ~$200 million ongoing benefit to the company’s cash flow.

Management plans to use this cash inflow “prudently.” I read this to mean that investors should expect Delek to work down the excess cash position over time, rather than immediately. Additionally, some cash will be devoted to debt reduction and not just share buybacks; still, the company’s financial flexibility is greatly enhanced. Delek has $265 million of stand-alone net debt, and I expect DK to eventually target a ~neutral net debt posture.

DK has significant 2026 cash flow, enabling buybacks

On January 13th, Delek released a brief 2026 update. Most notably, it expects $200-200 million of cap-ex and turnaround spending. This includes a Q1 turnaround, which temporarily reduces throughput. Given the weaker crack spread environment, this is an opportune time for such work. Beyond that, the company has a new inventory intermediation agreement, which should unlock about $40 million of cash. Including its distributions from DKL, DK should generate at least $400 million of cash flow that can be distributed to investors in 2026, even in a ~$20 crack spread environment.

It is also worth noting that alongside Q3 earnings, Delek increased the expected benefits of cost-cutting program from $150 to $180 million. The combination of its cost-saving program and SRE relief have removed over $300 million from the company’s prior cost structure. This is already enabling capital returns. During Q3, Delek repurchased $15 million of stock in addition to its dividend. With proceeds from the SRE decision, I see scope to increase buyback activity. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down about 5% from a year ago, and DK has steadily reduced its share count over time. Shares offer a 3.5% dividend yield.

Valuation makes DK a strong buy

When valuing Delek, it is important to remember that the company owns a ~33.9 million share position in Delek Logistics Partners (DKL). This stake is worth just over $1.6 billion, and it also provides DK with about $150 million of annual cash flow from DKL’s distributions. This stake is worth over $26 per share, meaning that the rest of Delek is being valued at about $250 million at $30, alongside its net $265 million of cash. This entire $515 million enterprise value is covered by the 2026 DKL distribution of $150 million and $400 million of SRE cash flow from the EPA’s ruling. That value’s Delek’s refineries as essentially zero.

Frankly, I think investors are focused too much on near-term gasoline inventories and ignoring the bigger picture on a valuation basis. Even at $100 million of free cash flow per year (giving no credit for its cost saving program or DKL distributions), DK has a stand-alone equity value of ~$15/share at 10x free cash flow. Combined with its stake in DKL, that is a fair value of $40. I see fair value closer to $25, given my view that free cash flow is closer to $200 million ex-DKL and haircutting its cost-cutting program partially, or $50 all-in.

At $30, markets are ascribing no enterprise value to DK’s wholly owned businesses, factoring in 2026 cash inflows and its DKL stake. This has created a significant margin of safety, even in a weak refining market. With 25+% upside, I view DK as one of the most compelling value stocks in the market, and as such, I am upgrading shares back to a “strong buy” on their pullback, with 2026 buybacks likely to help push shares towards fair value.

data.title: Duolingo: Finally Worthy Of An Entry
data.description:
Duolingo, Inc. (DUOL) is the leading language learning app, gaining a significant market share through successful media presence and engaging app content. Earnings have followed Duolingo's operational metrics with strong growth and profitability.

Yet, since having an IPO in 2021, Duolingo's road on the stock market has been mixed. The stock previously surged alongside user metrics and earnings, but concerns have started to arise from mid-2025 forward. Widespread user backlash and Duolingo's previously very expensive stock valuation have led the stock to plummet. I believe that a lower stock price now represents an attractive opportunity.

Long-Term Growth Story Is Great

Contrary to Duolingo's recent stock performance, the overall growth story has been successful. Daily active users have grown from 10.1 million in late 2021 to 50.5 million currently at a significantly rapid 53.6% CAGR, capturing a massive market share in the space. While very small compared to Meta Platforms' (META) 3.54 billion daily users, for a language learning app, the number of users is massive. The number of monthly active users has reached 135.3 million.

Financials have followed Duolingo's user growth. Revenues are nearing the billion-dollar level for the past twelve months, and EBITDA has scaled from near break-even levels in 2021 to a 29.5% margin in Q3'2025 through operating leverage. A capital light business model has enabled Duolingo to also generate significantly high free cash flow, reaching $272 million for the past twelve months. Monetization of the app has improved stably - paid subscriber penetration has improved from 6.2% at the end of 2021 to 9.0% currently.

A lot of Duolingo's success can be traced to its exceptional brand marketing strategy. The company is known for its green owl mascot and "unhinged" social media strategy that caters especially to younger generations, driving media impressions and ultimately converting into active users. Duolingo's brand has gained massive recognition from social media posts, cementing the brand as a leader in the language learning app space. Duolingo has 17 million followers on TikTok and nearly 4.8 million followers on Instagram. While a lot of corporations' efforts to cater to younger generations through memes is often seen as tacky or gimmicky, Duolingo has been able to dodge the label effectively.

Another reason for Duolingo's success seems to be in the app's interactivity. Duolingo has been able to "gamify" language learning, making the app more engaging to use and ultimately improving user retention.

Earnings momentum hasn't slowed down yet. Duolingo still reported a 34% increase in paid subscribers in Q3, leading to 41% revenue growth and a 480 basis point gain in the EBITDA margin. Quarterly EBITDA came in at $80 million. The language learning app space is still growing and changing, making TAM estimates challenging, but Duolingo has at least so far proven its ability to continue growing its user reach. Duolingo is expanding to additional learning opportunities in chess, math, and music, creating additional long-term potential.

AI Backlash Still Lingers

The growth story isn't without caveats. Duolingo is still tackling with previous brand backlash, creating a more volatile future ahead for the brand.

Duolingo's CEO's April 2025 announcement that Duolingo will prioritize generative AI over human workers caused widespread backlash to the company. While trying to improve company efficiency and offer customers more learning content through AI, Duolingo's customer base took the announcement very negatively. Social media posts suddenly got flooded with criticism, even leading Duolingo to previously wipe out its TikTok and Instagram posts to prevent negativity around the brand.

Given the fact that a major reason for Duolingo's success has been in its social media strategy, the brand's more negative reception could turn out to be a large issue. Duolingo has since resumed "unhinged" social media posts during Q3, trying to move past the controversy, but social media engagement hasn't reached previous levels yet despite an increase in marketing expenses. Relevancy on TikTok is still clearly weaker than previously as is suggested by a low number of likes on the platform, and Duolingo is even slowly losing followers on Instagram in early 2026. The marketing funnel needs to reaccelerate.

For now, the app's growth has slowed down but hasn't even nearly halted. Duolingo announced preliminary metrics from Q4, reporting approximately 30% DAU growth and at least 24% growth in bookings. The company seems to still have added around 2.2 million daily active users during Q4 alone despite lower social media engagement, slowing down from previous levels but remaining at a good pace.

The longer-term financial impact remains to be seen. Without reacceleration, lower social media engagement is ultimately weakening Duolingo's ability to attract new users. Duolingo's final Q4 results should come in late February with an initial 2026 guidance, giving important insight into Duolingo's continued performance. Given that Duolingo has still continued to attract new users throughout 2025, I believe that growth will likely slow down but remain at a good pace. Duolingo's brand recognition remains very strong.

Positively, the controversy is becoming more distant - the AI backlash could ultimately be forgotten after some time. Facebook initially reported a slight hiccup in user growth for a couple of quarters amid the 2018 Cambridge Analytica scandal, but resumed faster user growth afterwards. Duolingo could show a similar trend.

Duolingo's Valuation Is Now Too Cheap

I built a discounted cash flow model to estimate a fair value for Duolingo.

In a base scenario, I assume growth to continue as Duolingo has continued to grow its user base and as long-term monetization creates further earnings potential. Yet, I do estimate a clear slowdown as increasing scale and AI backlash is making further growth more challenging to achieve. I estimate the 2024-2034 revenue CAGR at 11.6% with 3% perpetual growth after the estimate period.

Revenue growth and AI efficiencies should create potential for some further margin expansion. I estimate the EBITDA margin to ultimately reach 33%.

For now, Duolingo's growth makes the company's cash flow conversion incredibly good - subscriptions that have been paid in advance create a discrepancy between bookings and revenue, and the discrepancy will remain as long as revenue continues to grow. I estimate the cash flow conversion to weaken gradually as growth slows down, remaining at a good level throughout the estimate period.

I assume 10% dilution to Duolingo's current outstanding shares to offset the company's significant amount of stock-based compensation.

The estimates put Duolingo's fair value at $215.5, suggesting 33% upside as of writing. Even when modeling a significant slowdown in growth, the stock is clearly undervalued. Duolingo isn't priced for very much earnings growth anymore, leaving the stock with the cheapest earnings multiples in its history on the market.

The forward EV/EBITDA only stands at 21.5, a low multiple for a company that has continued to report good double-digit growth in key metrics alongside margin expansion.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.99%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don't assume Duolingo to draw any debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.20% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran's most recent January estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance estimates Duolingo's beta at 0.86. With a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 7.99%.

Takeaway

Duolingo's growth story has faced a roadblock - previous investor enthusiasm has turned into fear. While key metrics have continued to grow, the market is now clearly afraid of slowing user growth as previous AI backlash still lingers. I believe that while such concerns are warranted, the decline has gone too far - DUOL stock is priced attractively given the company's financial outlook. I initiate DUOL at a Buy rating.

data.title: BLCR: Imperfect Risk-Adjusted Returns And Liquidity Weigh On The Rating, A Hold
data.description:
With today's note, I would like to initiate coverage of the iShares Large Cap Core Active ETF (BLCR), with the Hold rating.

BLCR had an outstanding 2025, with a total return of 30.93%, 13.75% larger than that of the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), so it, of course, might seem at first blush at least a bit bizarre that I am for some reason ignoring this achievement and not assigning a Buy rating to it.

However, even though it would be my pleasure to initiate the discussion with a bullish thesis, there are strong arguments to support my choice of the neutral tone.

  • The first one is that zooming out, we see that its performance no longer looks that convincing, as since its inception in October 2023, BLCR has underperformed the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) and IWB in the last months of 2023 and in 2024, plus its risk-adjusted returns over November 2023–December 2025 were not particularly appealing, as it captured about 117.8% of IWB's downside.
  • The second one is that its assets under management of $113.14 million that it has amassed over more than 26 months of its existence are rather low. And its 30-day median bid/ask spread (as of January 13) of 0.14% does deserve to be called rather wide and fairly uncomfortable.

Obviously, there are other details that deserve to be addressed. So as usual, in the article, I will be walking my dear readers through the essential aspects of this vehicle, including strategy, performance, and portfolio composition, paying special attention to the chief concerns, which persuaded me that justification for a bullish thesis is absent, though the data to present a bearish view is lacking either.

BLCR Key Facts

  • Inception: October 24, 2023
  • AUM: $113.14 million, as of January 13
  • Turnover: 94%
  • Net Expense Ratio: 0.36%

BLCR Strategy

As explained on the iShares website, BLCR is an actively managed ETF that "...seeks to maximize total return by investing primarily in large capitalization U.S. equities identified through fundamental and quantitative insights."

As mentioned in the product brief, BLCR

...seeks to marry a long-term view on company earnings with valuation to construct a high conviction, fundamentally-driven portfolio. Stock specific holdings are unconstrained by style, while the portfolio seeks to achieve consistent large cap blend exposure.

How are large caps defined? As elaborated on page S-2 of the prospectus,

...large cap equity securities are equity securities that, at the time of purchase, have a market capitalization within the range of companies included in the Russell 1000® Index (the “Russell 1000 Index”).

Besides, BLCR can venture into international equities as well:

The Fund invests primarily in common stock of U.S. companies, but the Fund may invest up to 25% of its total assets in the securities of foreign companies and ADRs.

Also, the BLCR portfolio may include derivatives, preferred stock, and convertible securities.

Another essential part of the strategy is that the fund may radically recalibrate its portfolio amid challenging market conditions:

During temporary defensive periods (i.e., in response to adverse market, economic or political conditions), the Fund may depart from its principal investment strategies and may invest up to 100% of its total assets in liquid, short-term investments, including high quality, short-term securities.

How active is BLCR in adding and removing holdings from its portfolio? We see that the fund has a rather high turnover of 94%, but I would not say that it is reshuffling its holdings incessantly. The fact is, I delved a bit deeper and took a look at the holdings dataset as of August 7, 2025, which I downloaded using the Wayback Machine. Comparing it with the dataset as of January 9 from the ETF's website, I found out that since then, BLCR has removed just three stocks, namely the following:

Stock Weight as of August 7, 2025
Mr. Cooper Group Inc. (traded with a ticker COOP) 3.79%
Boston Scientific Corporation (BSX) 2.61%
Synopsys, Inc. (SNPS) 2.05%

Data from the ETF's Detailed Holdings and Analytics file. Downloaded using the Wayback Machine

In the meantime, only seven names were added. So holdings that were in the portfolio in August account for about 84.7% of it in January, which means that over certain periods, changes may not be as profound as the fiscal year turnover rate suggests.

Stock Weight as of January 9, 2026
Rocket Companies, Inc. (RKT) 3.41%
WESCO International, Inc. (WCC) 2.84%
Intel Corporation (INTC) 2.07%
Medtronic plc (MDT) 1.84%
Rentokil Initial plc (RTO) 1.59%
Snowflake Inc. (SNOW) 1.50%
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) 0.57%

Data from the ETF

BLCR Returns

A 30.93% total return in 2025 is nothing short of spectacular. However, the issue here is that longer-term performance does not look that convincing.

As we know from the fact sheet, BLCR is benchmarked against the Russell 1000 index, so it makes sense to add IWB to the comparison, together with IVV, which I usually use as a market proxy, and the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ).

The chart above illustrates that BLCR has done a solid job since its inception, beating both IWB and IVV, yet this was chiefly the result of its 2025 performance (and in 2024, it underperformed IWB by 7.24%). At the same time, it trailed QQQ.

The next table (covering the November 2023–December 2025 period) gives even more food for thought, chiefly in terms of risk metrics.

  • BLCR captured about 117.8% of IWB's downside, which is quite a weak result.
  • Its risk-adjusted returns (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios) over the period were the weakest in the group because of its high volatility.

BLCR Portfolio Analysis

What is inside the BLCR portfolio at the moment? Let us start with a brief review of the composition and then proceed to the factor mix.

First and foremost, it is concentrated, with just 36 equities as of January 9. Other asset classes are also represented, including cash and a money market fund, though their combined weight is just 1.53%.

As to the overlap with the IVV portfolio, it is quite significant, with most of BLCR's holdings being the S&P 500 constituents, as shown in the table below.

Metric BLCR IVV
Stocks present in both BLCR and IVV 81.67% 39.43%

Calculated by the author using data from the ETFs

Looking at the key ten holdings (47.1% of the net assets), we see that BLCR puts emphasis not only on the trillion-dollar league that most Large Blend and Large Growth ETFs favor but also on names that have either diminutive weights in IWB and IVV or are ignored by them, like Ciena Corporation (CIEN), one of the leading players in the high-speed connectivity arena.

BLCR Sector Exposures

In terms of sectors, BLCR is anything but copying allocations of IWB and IVV. In fact, while it is similarly heavy in IT, it is different in many ways. For instance, consumer staples, utilities, and real estate are ignored completely.

On a side note, the absence of the former two hints that, at least at this point, the fund sees the possibility of a large market correction or a recession as rather low, which means there is no need for defensive allocations.

BLCR Factor Mix

What is unique about the BLCR portfolio's factor mix? The essential fact is that the emphasis is clearly put on the growth factor (reinforced with momentum) while maintaining a sufficient footprint (but not outstanding) in quality stocks. The factors that it pays little attention to are value and low volatility, with nuances though.

As to these nuances, BLCR has a larger allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or higher. But its adjusted weighted average earnings yield is still much more compressed.

Regarding the low volatility factor, we see that the weighted average beta coefficients of its portfolio are rather high, yet BLCR still has exposure to 17 stocks (a 36.7% weight) with a 24-month beta coefficient of less than 1.

Of course, its focus on growth and momentum creates certain risks: considering that the market is fairly high priced, I should warn that any negative surprises related to inflation, interest rates, and AI capex (or, as I sometimes say, the "AI ROI") can trigger a considerable drawdown for longer-duration equities, and BLCR, which is enamored of them, will definitely suffer.

But what about the GARP characteristics?

Here, we see that the BLCR portfolio has lower PSG and EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratios, but the ETFs' PEG ratios are almost on par, so I would not construct a GARP thesis here.

Final Thoughts

So all in all, BLCR's active strategy based on "fundamental and quantitative insights" definitely had a moment last year, delivering a total return that dwarfs quite easily not only that of IWB but also QQQ (20.77%, to be precise), which is quite a solid achievement, as QQQ is bizarrely hard to just keep pace with in bull markets, let alone to outmatch by such a massive margin (10.16%).

However, I believe more arguments are needed to construct a bullish view. Indeed, BLCR is an interesting vehicle offering some unorthodox exposures, but it would be nice to see higher risk-adjusted returns and the downside capture ratio not that large. Besides, liquidity-wise, there is room for improvement, especially as far as the median bid/ask spread is concerned.

data.title: SoundHound AI Has A Lot Of Growth Opportunities Despite Apparent Risks
data.description:
Introduction

SoundHound AI (SOUN) is a speech recognition and voice AI platform that focuses on providing conversational tier AI models for businesses to embed into their products and services - such as drive-through ordering systems and customer service centers. SOUN aims to perform like a human does - by understanding user intent, grammar, tone, and meaning in real-world events.

SOUN is a buy - its service is providing a unique angle to the future of agentic commerce, and some of the largest names in the industry are backing them. In the below section we will review some highlights and macro trends related to SOUN.

Highlights and Trends

Growth has been stellar

The firm has experienced strong growth from new customers and AI increasing in popularity ever since the launch of ChatGPT in 2022. See the below graph from the firm's November 2025 Investor Presentation that reviews their platform clients since their inception in 2005.

The firm is showing clear signs of traction increasing amongst their users, and is experiencing hockey-stick-like growth as AI has increased in usage globally.

Their wide range of partner firms and customers shows their reach and brand strength - many firms across multiple industries are using SOUN services to power their intelligent AI talking abilities. Major use cases are shown below.

We believe that the firm has finally gotten to a point where the market is in dire need of their product. It has taken a while, but their revenue growth, as discussed in the following section, clearly showcases their vast opportunity.

Voice AI TAM is growing

The overall TAM for AI voice agents has increased significantly and is forecasted to grow quickly into the future. The projected total TAM by 2034 is over $47.5B, and SOUN should be able to capture a meaningful piece of this pie. We believe that SOUN's existing portfolio of products allows them to innovate quickly by capturing data from their existing client base to hone their system even further. This positive flywheel effect should lead to the firm being able to increase their presence in this space and expand into the future. At CES 2026, the firm revealed the launch of its Amelia 7 platform for smart cars, TVs, and other smart home devices that will help them to perform tasks such as ordering food, making reservations, booking travel, and more. This is done in collaboration with Nvidia, who is the leader in the AI industry. This will help them grow their royalties business further, which is the division that supplies third parties with voice AI systems where SOUN gets paid on volume, users, and active usage. Additionally, Affirm collects revenue on orders and purchases conducted through their platform, which means that increasing their overall portfolio is directly beneficial to their revenue. This is a positive for the firm in our view.

Agentic AI growth

According to a McKinsey study, nearly 62% of organizations are looking to adopt some form of AI agent in the near term. This naturally would lead to increasing exposure and need for voice-AI capabilities and should help boost the profile of SOUN further as use cases continue to increase. Further, as AI becomes more and more human-like, the next step beyond being able to answer and respond to input is having real-time adaptive thinking and reasoning across all situations and environments and to output like a human would. This is where SOUN should be able to stand out.

See this below image from Deloitte that shares how AI will shift in just 3 years.

We believe that SOUN is positioned strongly to provide increased value in reasoning and planning, especially within natural language processing and understanding. The macro environment is supportive of SOUN's business, which we believe is a bullish sign.

Financials

To understand the financial health of SOUN, we look at trends in key metrics.

Note: Since the data from the latest full year (2024) is now over 1 year old, we also include the TTM stats, which are current as of September 2024. Keep in mind that the 2024 numbers and the TTM numbers have 1 quarter of overlap.

Starting with revenue, we see a strong positive trend, with at least 1.47x growth each year. Ignoring the TTM value, we are seeing a CAGR of 58.7% between 2021 and 2024. If you include the TTM as its own year, the CAGR comes out to 62.7%

This kind of revenue trend is great to see - the company has consistently high growth without any downward fluctuation.

The net income picture looks a bit different. While revenue has continued to grow, the company's net income has an overall negative trend, with 2024 and the TTM showing exceptionally large losses compared to other years. In 2024, this was in large part due to a $222.6M expense in the 2024 income statement for adjustments of fair value due to recent acquisitions. The TTM number is impacted by similar adjustments. Additionally, there was the $80 million acquisition of Amelia that occurred in Q3 2024.

Keeping these impacts in consideration, we see that the 2024 net income related to the core operations of the business is not as bad as it initially seems. However, we would not discount the impacts fully. The adjustments related to recent acquisitions are due to contingent earnouts of stock. As the company's stock price increases, the liability will also increase, and these will result in shareholder dilution when they come due.

Valuation

Given that SOUN is a high-growth, still negative-earnings company, we utilize a P/S peer comparison to understand their relative valuations. We look at some of the peers of the company based on similar industries/products.

At first glance, it looks like SOUN is extremely overvalued compared to its peers. However, we should also factor in revenue growth. Below is the 3 year CAGR of revenue growth of each company:

As we see, SOUN blows the rest of the list out of the water when it comes to revenue growth. If we see a similar trend continue, in 1 year SOUN's P/S ratio (assuming the same current price) will have dropped by a factor of 1.77x to 16.66 in 1 year. Another year later it would be 9.41. We see that the P/S value for SOUN is projected to quickly catch up with the rest. Other than AI, the other peers are seeing negative revenue trends that will push their P/S valuations higher.

Let's put these into more concrete numbers. Assuming that the 3 year CAGR is representative of the future growth of the companies, we see that in 3 years the P/S ratios look like so:

SOUN is still overvalued compared to most of the list. However, the difference is much tighter. The average P/S of the competitors now is 6.02. Applying this valuation to SOUN, we see a 13% upside on their valuation. At a current price of $11.28, that would imply a value of $12.75.

Overall, we believe that even with the adjustments and projections, the 13% upside is an underestimation. It is based off of the company's past growth and does not account for the upcoming catalysts of AI adoption and benefits from current wide adoption.

Risks

The Company has several risk factors

When opening the SOUN page on Seeking Alpha, we are greeted with a big red banner stating, "Warning: SOUN is at high risk of performing badly." When looking deeper at the numbers reported, there are some potential concerns, including highly negative cash from operations, net incomes, and other margins. While some of these are concerning, the net income-based metrics are a bit misleading, as they are based on non-cash expenses related to acquisition liabilities, as we discussed above. When SOUN does need to pay these out, it will also be a non-cash expense, as it is stock compensation. Conversely, we do note that while it is a non-cash expense, it may still negatively impact shareholders via dilution. If the company continues its growth trajectory, this might not be a very large issue.

Competition is intense

There are tons of players operating in the voice AI space, as the sheer growth of the industry is immense, as covered in earlier sections. This means that there are certainly a number of competitors that are targeting the same customers as SOUN, which makes it incredibly important that they execute on their goals. While we believe that the AI landscape is large enough for multiple winners, SOUN needs to prove to the investor community that they are able to consistently grow top and bottom lines while improving efficiency.

Conclusion

SOUN has an interesting platform in the voice AI industry that is growing quickly and innovating rapidly. Their integration with customers in a wide variety of industries shows that they are gaining traction and that their service has value. In a market with an increasing TAM, such as voice AI, there is room for numerous winners, and SOUN could be one of them. Investing in SOUN as a speculative component of your portfolio could prove to be a winner in the future.

The revenue/earnings stats for the company are a bit mixed. On one hand, we see strong and growing revenue, but on the other hand, we are seeing large net losses, especially in recent years. As we discussed, the large net losses might not be a big risk to the company itself but could be a possible dilution event for investors.

In all, we see a lot of growth opportunity for the company, so do not expect the dilution issue to be a problem for shareholders. We rate SOUN as a buy.

data.title: V2X: The Surprising Winner Of The Aerospace And Defense Surge
data.description:
V2X (VVX) had a strong start to the year, gaining more than 20% on the back of a possible $1.5 trillion defense budget. The stock is now up 13.5% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500’s 9.3% gain. In this report, I discuss whether the stock still has upside after the recent surge and update my price target in support of my buy rating.

A Surprising Outperformance For V2X

Looking at the performance of V2X, I have to say it's a positive surprise to see V2X outperform aerospace and defense peers. The S&P 500 gained around 2%, so the aerospace and defense industry saw strong outperformance, gaining 16.7%. V2X had an even stronger gain at around 20.1%. Historically, V2X has been an underperformer, and to understand why that is the case, we have to understand the business itself.

V2X Is A Lower-Margin Mission-Support And Services Contractor

V2X is not a company that produces weapon systems. Instead, the company fundamentally is a services and mission-support contractor, which means the company bids for contracts for base operations, logistics, sustainment and training. Generally, this is a business with low margins. V2X has margins of around 7%. The margin used to be around but below 8%. So, the company has struggled to keep margins steady.

Mission-support contractors usually face high costs, inflating the revenues, but the actual profits are not big. Part of the reason for that is not just the high cost nature of the business that V2X is in. One element that limits the margins is the fact that many of these contracts are so called “cost-plus” contracts, which are contracts on which the contract value is structured as cost coverage plus a fee. That means that the risk of higher than anticipated costs remains with the customer, and in exchange, the margin on those contracts is smaller, since the cost risk is also lower. That's a stark contrast from fixed-cost contracts, where operational efficiency defines the margins, commanding an overall higher margin opportunity. V2X typically has cost-plus work for base operations in the Middle East.

Additional pressures include the initial ramp up of contracts as well as winding down programs and recompetes on contracts. A recompete is when an existing contract with the government ends and the government opens up the contract for bidding. Since these often are long-term contracts, mission-support contractors are eager to bid for these contracts since the next opportunity for the same program could be years later, and that means that even if V2X did a terrific job on the first contract, it still has to put in a competitive offer.

A solution for contracts on which V2X is performing well would be to convert the existing or new contracts into fixed-cost contracts. However, that's easier said than done. Remaining on a cost-plus type contract gives the government more space to add scope to the contract, cut risks of underperformance on the contracted work to lower costs and optimize margins, and maintain negotiation strength for recompetes. Additionally, for V2X going with a cost-plus nature may also not be desired due to volatile cost components such as labor, fuel, scope, and subcontractor pricing, which are harder to predict over the longer term. For that reason, margins on the contracts and, consequently, margins for mission-support providers are lower.

For aerospace and defense names, I'm typically looking for margins of 10%-15%. For mission-support contractors, that would be 8%-9%. In some way it's quite surprising to see a lower-margin business see its stock outperform higher-margin peers.

Higher Defense Spending Has an Indirect Benefit for Mission-Support Contractors

That V2X stock benefited as much as it did from the prospects of higher defense spending also does not make a lot of sense if you look at what a higher defense budget would result in. It would definitely not result in higher fees on sustainment. The rule of thumb is that 50% of the life cycle cost of a defense platform goes to R&D and procurement, and the remainder is procurement. That provides another reason as to why sustainment contracts have lower margins. It's where the government can keep some control on cost without compromising on capability developed and procurement quantities.

Typically, for a defense budget, around 40% of the budget is allocated to sustainment. It can be expected that a higher defense budget would be leveraged to advance research and development, as we have seen the share of R&D growth go from the low teens to high teens over the past decade. Since we're currently at a point where tension is high and capabilities and threat levels require advancement in hypersonics, cyber, drone, and missile air defense. So, additional defense funds in the budget would likely be earmarked to advance and accelerate capability development without development programs cannibalizing each other due to budget constraints. Down the line, additional funding would prevent programs from cannibalizing procurement funds. We have seen that F-35 procurement quantities have come down simply because there are not enough dollars available in the budget to procure as initially intended. That means that before we see any strong benefit for mission-support contractors, we will see more funds flowing to the development of new capabilities and accelerating that development and, after that, procurement. Mission-support and services in some way is last in line to benefit, and the benefit would be more in out-years rather than in the near term.

Rather, the benefit from a higher defense budget would come from the potential of having more defense platforms in service and higher quantities of defense equipment in service. All will require sustainment work to be carried out. However, that spike in the defense budget will not substantially alter the “work available” for services contractors, as output will not rise immediately. So, there's a multi-step and multi-year process required for the benefit to flow to service providers. There would only be a direct benefit for companies such as V2X if military activity increases in the near term for a prolonged period, requiring additional maintenance to be carried out. That would be more of a volume-driven growth driver than a margin opportunity.

V2X Remains Attractive Despite Lower Nature Business

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For V2X, I'm adjusting my base case price target from $67.45 to $80.70 as we shift the valuation window from 2025 to 2026. We note that compared to my prior assessment, the target for 2025 was reduced by $0.20, driven by lower expectations on free cash flow. EBITDA estimates have remained stable with a 0.6% upward revision for 2025-2027, indicating 5.5% growth. Free cash flow estimates came down by more than 25% for 2025, driven by higher working capital requirements, and have also come down by 12.5% for this year and almost 10% for next year.

EBITDA is expected to dip in 2025 to around 7% and recover in the years after. We also see the dip back in the free cash flow performance and a higher share of capital expenditures against the operating cash flow. V2X has a $100 million repurchase program, which I believe the company will carry out over the next few years. We also note that the company is expected to grow its cash position in the years ahead and have a leverage below 2x this year. For aerospace and defense companies, I target a net-debt leverage of 2x-2.5x. That means that, in theory, V2X has around $255 million to $455 million to spend on acquisitions by 2027 or return that value to shareholders.

Conclusion: V2X Buy Rating Is Maintained

V2X is the surprising winner of the surge in aerospace and defense stock prices. Looking at the nature of the business, we note that V2X would not be the straightforward winner because a prospective increase in the defense budget would predominantly benefit original equipment manufacturers and not so much the mission-support contractors such as V2X. Nonetheless, we note that with the stock price increase, the company is now more or less fairly valued against 2025 earnings, and additional upside exists for this year, supporting my buy rating.

data.title: L3Harris Technologies: Too Important To Fail, But Not A Buy At Any Price
data.description:
The $1 billion agreement between the U.S. Department of Defense and L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX) represents one of the fundamental drivers that are changing the positive outlook for this company and for the industry. It gives the company a high level of control over the long-term future and also establishes a degree of visibility and a backstop of government support to protect the long-term position of a key asset for this company.

For me there was no question about that. I have owned shares of L3Harris since before this news, and I believe the structural picture of the company has been enhanced. However, the reaction of the market to the announcement of the agreement has been quick. Possibly too quick. The price of the stock is at historic highs, the stock has tremendous momentum (more on that later), and the profit taking is completely normal.

Therefore, while I still believe the stock is a Strong Buy, the timing of entry into the stock is more important than ever.

The Pentagon Agreement

As I previously stated in my August article, I delivered an in-depth overview of the company and its strategic position. For those readers who wish to understand the entire picture of L3Harris's business and its strategic position, I would recommend reading that article again. However, for purposes of this analysis, I want to highlight what is new in the terms of the agreement with the Pentagon and why this agreement is relevant today.

What is significant about the agreement with the Pentagon is not the $1 billion by the Pentagon to L3Harris, but rather the form of the participation of the Pentagon. This is not simply another contract or a supplemental order: the Department of Defense is directly participating, through convertible preferential equity, in the new entity that will be created from the spinoff of the Missile Solutions business. In effect, the Pentagon is partnering with L3Harris as a supplier, in addition to purchasing products from L3Harris.

In other words, the Pentagon will become a partner of L3Harris in the new company. The new company will be focused on producing solid rocket motors for various systems, including Tomahawk, SM-6, Patriot, and THAAD missile defense systems. L3Harris will retain approximately 51-60% of the ownership of the new company and will go public through an IPO in the second half of 2026.

In return for this investment, the Pentagon will receive a high level of visibility related to their requirements for these types of systems. Specifically, the Pentagon will obtain framework contracts with the new company for up to 10 years, and estimates call for the volume of solid rocket motors purchased to range from 600 to 2,000 units annually, which can be translated into terms of the size of the opportunity and the value of the agreement to both parties.

In essence, the Pentagon is saying that this production capacity is so important that it cannot fall prey to the typical ebbs and flows of an industry. The supply chain shortages that occurred as a result of war in Ukraine and the Middle East have made evident just how small the Western industrial base is. This thinking is clearly proactive; invest now to avoid bottlenecks later. With the government involved in the capital, with demand guarantees, and with a project included in the broader framework of the approximately $140 billion "Golden Dome" missile defense project, the risk-return profile of the propulsion business has changed substantially. On paper, it looks very attractive. I may be mistaken, but this is one of the first times I've seen a real "socialization" of risk without eliminating the private capital market. Of course, there will need to be execution, which makes the above somewhat uncomfortable due to potential conflicts of interest and regulatory oversight.

Not Just a Contract

This is something entirely different. The Department of Defense is actually purchasing the capital asset of the manufacturing process by making a $1 billion investment in the form of convertible preferred equity into Missile Solutions; which is the new entity to be spun off from L3Harris' solid rocket motor business. When I refer to Missile Solutions as "too important to fail," I do not mean that the U.S. Government is going to guarantee profitability to Missile Solutions or eliminate the risks associated with its operations; rather, it means that the Department of Defense is stating that solid rocket motor production capability is a strategic variable and that the Department of Defense cannot afford to lose such capability due to insufficient capital, excessive lead time between orders, and/or bottlenecks. Therefore, the Department of Defense has determined to participate in the equity financing of Missile Solutions through the use of convertible instruments. In essence, a separate entity (Missile Solutions) is being established where LHX will retain 51% ownership of the same, Missile Solutions plans to go public via an IPO during the second half of 2026, and until such time as the IPO occurs, Missile Solutions will utilize multi-year capital to expand its industrial base and enhance the reliability of its supply chain.

2026 (1H) Set-up of the new Missile Solutions entity and definition of governance/capex plan
2026 (2H) IPO planned (2H26) + details on shareholder structure and use of proceeds
2027 Ramp-up launch: first effects on production capacity and delivery times
2028-29 Increase in output towards a range of 600–2,000 engines/year; stabilization of the supply chain
2030 More "steady-state" capacity: scaled-up production + longer contractual visibility

To LHX, this is not a near-term increase in [EPS], however, it will provide a change in profile to the company since it will receive increased visibility, increased predictability, and, most importantly, reduced risk of being left behind with respect to structurally high demand, while having limited manufacturing capacity available. I may be mistaken, but I believe this is one of the few instances where I have seen a customer "put their money where their mouth is," so to speak, in order to secure future delivery of product.

A Better Story, But a More Demanding Entry Point

After announcing the story, the stock price immediately shot up and hit a new high. The market responded rapidly, values shifted, and, technically speaking, momentum began to develop. Now let’s get down to business; the better part of the good news has already been factored into the price of the stock.

However, if we look closely at the charts, we can't easily conclude with the usual comment, "You're paying too much." Yes, LHX is trading at a forward EV/EBITDA blended multiple of around 17.5x, significantly above the five-year historical average of just under 13x. It's clear to me that the market is placing greater importance on the visibility and quality of the company's future cash flows than in the past, as I mentioned earlier. This places greater demands on the stock in the short term because historically, similar levels of valuation typically result in a period of consolidation.

On the forward P/E multiple side, however, the situation is somewhat different. According to the data, LHX is still trading at a lower P/E multiple than the average of its peer group, which is trading at higher multiples (see the chart). Therefore, while the stock has become more expensive relative to its own history, it is not the most expensive stock within the sector, which helps to explain why the long term thesis continues to be valid even after the rapid increase in price.

Finally, there is the technical aspect of momentum. The RSI is in the overbought range, which does not indicate that the story itself is weak, but rather that short term timing becomes increasingly difficult. As stated earlier, the market anticipates strong earnings growth, particularly beginning in 2027, when the spinoff is operational and production capacity begins to expand. Thus, it is completely natural for the stock to experience profit taking or to trade laterally until then.

In this regard, it can get very uncomfortable. Being a Strong Buy based on your thesis does not automatically equate to a “buy at any price.” It is quite risky to buy a stock that has already been on a strong upward trend. As such, I believe it makes sense to build your position in a more measured manner, potentially through several tranches, as opposed to attempting to force an immediate entry into a stock that has risen to high levels.

Preview Of Earnings

I believe earnings will be another important catalyst to monitor, although I don't expect any major surprises. Rather than focusing on actual earnings per share (EPS) for the quarter, I'm focusing on the quality of the company's order book and its visibility into the future of its product pipeline.

There are three key points that I will be watching in the upcoming quarterly earnings announcement:

  1. Production Capacity and Ramp-up Times: This is where the impact of the Pentagon deal is expected to start being felt, although the comment is likely to be qualitative (i.e., what the company says regarding production capacity and ramp-up times).
  2. Capital Discipline: How management describes capital expenditures, returns on investment, and priorities in light of the fact that the U.S. Government is becoming an industrial partner to the company.
  3. International Demand: Particularly NATO and export opportunities, which continue to represent a strong structural opportunity with a high degree of non-cyclicality.

At this point, expectations become important. The biggest risk after the recent rally is not a "bad" quarterly earnings report, but rather an average or normal quarterly report. While the market may be looking for immediate evidence of upward momentum in earnings, I believe that as long as there is no damage to medium- to long-term visibility, the noise created by short-term events is less significant.

Risk Factors

  1. Execution Risk of the Spinoff: I believe the process will run smoothly, and we can expect no issues with the implementation of the plan; however, as this is a "binary" event; I am viewing this factor with caution. There are several key factors that need to fall into place before the IPO (scheduled for the second half of 2026): Governance, Timing, Authorizations, Capital Allocation.
  2. Industrial vs. Political/Regulatory Risk: While having the Pentagon as our Partner significantly mitigates industrial risk; it does increase political/regulatory risk. When an organization becomes Strategic Infrastructure, you receive greater scrutiny, potentially limiting your ability to make executive decisions, and decreased flexibility regarding operations.
  3. Timing Risk: Investor sentiment has been raised due to recent rallies. Therefore, a quarterly earnings report that meets the expectations of investors, comments made by management regarding timelines for ramping up to full production, or the need for the company to consolidate its current price levels will create short term volatility. Timing risk will not prevent the long-term thesis from being correct; however, it will test investors who have entered this opportunity without sufficient margin to absorb potential losses.

Conclusion and Rating

All things considered, my view on L3Harris remains unchanged. The long-term thesis has become stronger, not weaker. Visibility has increased from an agreement with the Department of Defense (Pentagon), which also eliminates significant industrial risks to a very important component of their business. Additionally, L3Harris' strategic position relative to the U.S. government and its various agencies in the event of future geopolitical tensions has been enhanced, as the environment is less likely to return to normalcy for some time.

However, the market has acted very aggressively, and the stock is trading at all-time highs, and multiples are high. Therefore, while the opportunity to buy into L3Harris is still available, the required discipline is significantly greater than what was needed six months ago.

Therefore, my rating remains a Strong Buy. I believe that the combination of backlog, the long-term visibility of L3Harris, and the additional optionality provided by the planned spin-off of Missile Solutions supports the valuation premium being applied to the company today. I am maintaining a target price of $383 and anticipate a slow and steady rerating of the stock as Missile Solutions' industrial progress becomes increasingly clear.

data.title: PDI: A 15% Yielding Flagship Ready For The Rate-Cut Cycle
data.description:
Investment thesis

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) closed-end fund is, to my mind, currently one of the best assets for a personal investment portfolio focused on generating passive income. Here, several advantages are combined: high dividend yield (about 15%), wide diversification of the asset portfolio, adaptability to NAV growth during a cycle of falling interest rates, and a constant share price premium. Such characteristics of CEF make it a "Buy" recommendation, particularly if you are looking to diversify your portfolio by adding different types of assets. This article aims to analyze the concept of the PDI investment strategy and identify the main factors for the fund's potential growth in 2026. Also in this article, we will assess dividend safety, showing how the Fed's current monetary policy affects NII and the Coverage Ratio.

PDI's strategy and growth catalysts

The PDI is a closed-end fund whose investment strategy is based on working with debt instruments in the multi-sector market. Its main assets are mortgage-backed securities (MBS), derivatives, and high-yield bonds. According to the fund's prospectus, at least 25% of its assets are invested in private mortgage securities, and up to 40% in securities issued by economically developing countries. The structure of PDI's portfolio is such that 24.40% of its assets are high-yield credit, with 21.82% in non-agency mortgages and 14.94% in non-USD developed securities. In total, there are 2,037 holdings in the portfolio, and the top 10 assets account for 27.19%.

The distinctive feature of such a large-scale PDI portfolio is that it was formed mainly through the acquisition of another fund in the PIMCO family, namely PCI. As a result, a lot of the assets that were sold off and never realized remain in the PDI portfolio. In spite of their low liquidity, these assets were purchased at a very low price. Before the acquisition, most of them had lost around 90-95% of their value. Despite their outdated issue dates, the assets remain stable, creating a kind of “safety cushion.” The low price makes it difficult for these assets to depreciate further. On the contrary, the return on invested capital is quite high, enabling PDI to offer investors a current dividend yield of 14.54%.

In addition to its vast array of assets, PDI's competitive advantage lies in the size of its portfolio. Fund AUM currently stands at $7.20 billion. Taking PDI's two main competitors from the PIMCO family, Dynamic Income Opportunities Fund (PDO) and PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY), the combined value of their portfolios is 40% less. Not long ago, I compared one of them, the PTY, to its main competitor, the Guggenheim Strategic Opportunities Fund (GOF), where the recommendation is also "Buy." Coming back to the PDI, it’s worth noting that the size of its portfolio is important for attracting large institutional investors. Also, the liquidity in the market for PDI positions lets big money buy without much slippage in the order book. And the broad diversification of the portfolio provides protection against the potential default of one or more issuers, a definite advantage.

The scale of the portfolio requires a complex investment strategy, and in my opinion, that's another advantage of PDI. In addition to broad diversification, they use aggressive hedging through derivatives. The core of the fund's strategy is to aggressively use leverage and interest rate swaps. In periods of predictable interest rate changes, PDI generates excess returns, allowing it to make high payments to shareholders. Within the framework of credit leverage, the fund uses reverse repurchase agreements amounting to 29.62% of the total portfolio, and credit default swaps amounting to 1.42%, where the total effective leverage is 31.04%.

Proof of PDI's high interest among investors is that its shares are trading at a premium to NAV. At the moment, this premium is 7.13%. At the same time, the maximum PDI premium over the past year was on October 1, 2025, with a share price of $19.84 and a NAV of $17.02 (a premium of 14.21%). When I analyzed PDI in my previous article (in comparison with other funds in the PIMCO family), the premium was 4.3%. At that time, I saw it as a good opportunity to buy the asset for my investment portfolio. The share price has since risen by 1.68%, with a total return of 2.94%.

Reasons to invest in PDI in 2026

Now I am confident that PDI is a CEF worth adding to your investment portfolio. Analyzing the fundamental fund on the market and the economic situation in the US, here are the reasons why I recommend PDI as a “Buy”.

Reason #1. The high dividend yield

It's currently at 14.54%, and the average return over the last two years is around 14%. This yield previously reached an average of 17.81% in 2023. Such payments make PDI one of the most profitable funds with high performance and no significant capital erosion. Payments are made monthly, something that is very important for many income-oriented investors.

Reason #2. The cycle of rate cuts

Right now, Fed policy is all about lowering rates, and that's driving up the prices of bonds in PDI's portfolio. This has a positive impact on potential NAV growth. In addition, the financial stability of PDI, which is a CEF that actively uses leverage (31.04%), is dependent on interest rates in the credit market. And given the Fed's current cycle of rate cuts, there is also a decrease in the cost of servicing the leverage itself. This means a potential increase in profits for the fund.

Reason #3. Dynamics of P/NAV

With a current premium of 7.13%, buying PDI seems like a good deal. In general, such a moderate premium reflects the high assessment of investors who believe in the fund's portfolio profitability. Moreover, the total NAV return over the past year was more than 15%, demonstrating the effectiveness of portfolio managers in earning money on the market, and not just paying dividends from the capital.

Reason #4. Quality active management

I see the fact that the PDI portfolio remains unchanged not as a disadvantage, but as an advantage. It invests in a wide range of instruments, the majority of which are bonds and MBS. The portfolio's structure, in terms of weight, may change. Such a flexible approach allows it to adapt to changes in interest rates in order to maintain its high level of profitability. Asset duration averages more than three years. And buying them at an undervalued price generates the profits needed to pay dividends. What's more, PDI invests in assets that lock in previous high rates, allowing investments to become more profitable.

PDI dividend safety analysis: it's higher than you think

There is nothing more interesting to investors than whether the yield of around 15% is justified and whether it can be maintained given the current cycle of interest rate cuts. According to many, however, the size of net investment income could be affected. Furthermore, should it prove insufficient for current payments, the fund would have to use its own capital (RoC). This would cause erosion, and in my opinion, it would be imprudent for a fund such as PDI. Based on reports, Distribution Coverage Ratio will stabilize by the end of 2025. That's a good sign that the fund will be able to pay dividends to shareholders even without using its own capital.

PDI's leverage ratio also affects dividend security in 2026. Please note that the current Total Effective Leverage ratio is 31.04%, the main instrument being Reverse Repurchase Agreements. These instruments are linked to the SOFR rate. Therefore, every time the Fed cuts rates, repurchase rates also fall sharply. This results in a reduction in the cost of borrowing for the fund in a relatively short period of time. The SOFR rate is currently 3.64%, while at the beginning of September 2025 it was 4.39%. In absolute terms, the rate has fallen by 0.75%. As a percentage, that's a 17.08% drop. This means that PDI's borrowing costs could go down by about 17%, giving a nice boost to its margins. After all, many of its portfolio assets (especially MBS) have long durations. I think this is a strong internal catalyst for NAV growth and makes payments more reliable in the coming months.

When analyzing the data for 2025 in the Coverage Ratio section, note that PDI is going through a phase of restoring profitability. The coverage ratio fell to a critical 25.16% in May 2025. Since then, though, it's been steadily recovering, growing from $0.055 to $0.153. Payout coverage itself increased to 69.67% (latest data for November 2025). Growth in NII in the second half of 2025 coincides with changes in the Fed's monetary policy, underscoring the importance of the impact of lower borrowing costs through effective use of leverage. In addition, while the latest UNII figure is negative (-$0.45), investors should not be alarmed, since the fund managers are skillfully employing a derivatives strategy. This generates additional income that is not always reflected in the NII column.
